text: The electroreduction activity of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is driven by metastable surface features that exhibit unusually strong CO binding affinities. We demonstrate that OD-Cu electrodes, synthesized via the hydrogen reduction of $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ precursors, efficiently reduce CO to acetate and ethanol with nearly 50% Faradaic efficiency at moderate overpotentials. Using temperature-programmed desorption (TPD), we identified specific surface sites on OD-Cu with CO binding energies significantly higher than those found on the terraces or stepped sites of conventional polycrystalline copper foil. Thermal annealing at 350 °C leads to a 44-fold decrease in surface-area-normalized current density and a drop in Faradaic efficiency to below 5%. This degradation in performance directly correlates with the disappearance of these high-affinity CO binding sites. Our findings suggest that the exceptionally active sites for CO reduction on OD-Cu are associated with grain boundaries that stabilize strong CO adsorption. Characterizing these unique sites represents a critical step toward defining the surface chemistry required for the efficient electrochemical conversion of CO.
